physicalDetails.detail: Young human woman in practical travel robes with dusty hems, worn boots, and a braid half come loose from long field work.
Blue-eyed, visibly exhausted, with ink-stained fingers, callused hands, and frost-burned knuckles healed badly enough to show hard use of cold magic.
Chapter 2 keeps the field-worn look but sharpens it: throat scraped raw from repetition, road-grimed sleeves, stiff back, and burn-healed skin across the right hand that tightens when she curls it.
Her body reads strain even in motion — pins and needles in both legs when she stands too fast, headache pressure from the ward-circle, and abruptly unreliable legs after the wildlife fight.
By Chapter 3 the strain has gone visible in new ways: gone pale in house-light, sweat darkening the back of her neck in the granary, blue-white magic whitening the crystal and climbing her wrist.

Even when mounted again she carries fatigue in posture rather than surrender — staff across her thighs, later across the saddle bow, arms folded against the chill because the village has taken too much out of her.

Chapter 4 keeps the same travel-worn silhouette but makes fatigue more immediate: a travel-stained mage too tired for pride, bracing herself on tables and doorframes, throat gone dry from spellwork, and staff carried across both palms because her body no longer hides the cost.
Even under that strain she still moves first toward the evidence — into the granary, down over the spill, back through the hall to mark children and newer cases before she leaves.

Chapter 5 makes the wear in her body intimate and specific: hair fully escaped from its tie, ash under one thumb, grain dust at a cuff, purple fatigue under the eyes, and the kind of stiffness that only shows once the map work finally pauses.

Chapter 6 makes the cost of the investigation bodily and immediate: cold beading on her gloves, copied route page gripped against the pommel, fingers pressed to her brow, and hands shaking hard enough that she has to steady herself against her mare's neck after Arthas refuses her.

Chapter 7 keeps the field-worn strain but turns it into work posture: grain dust up both arms, sleeves rolled, one hand braced on the trestle while she reduces the city to residue boards, flare sheets, and treated cloth.

Chapter 8 keeps the field wear but turns it tactical: hidden between storehouses with ward-thread on her fingertips, coat jerked sideways by Sylvanas an instant before hostile magic takes the brick where her throat had been.

By the cellar scene her body records the chapter in stillness instead of collapse: she freezes at Kel'Thuzad's name, clamps the sending stone hard into her palm, then bends back over the route sheets with colder economy than before.
Chapter 9 puts her hood down and bare-fingered on the staff, then drops her into deep-analysis stillness so complete that Sylvanas has to drag her bodily back into the room when the ambush hits.
By the split she carries oilcloth-wrapped scraps, estate notes, and incomplete ritual data with a colder, lonelier field readiness than before.

Chapter 12 puts her in scholar-stillness instead of road strain: chalk dust on her thumb, both hands flattened on Kel'Thuzad's table, then a violent recoil that knocks the chair into stone when comprehension lands harder than fear.

The sanctum makes her body tell on her in small academic ways she hates noticing — picking up the chalk without thinking, wiping white dust onto her sleeve, forcing breath back into discipline while her legs threaten to go unreliable.

Chapter 13 catches Jaina in aftermath rather than approach: chalk on her left hand and sleeve, careful rigid joints, and the body of a woman holding still so hard it reads from the doorway as choice instead of damage.
On the exit and the boat she moves lightly enough to keep pace, but every landing and mount carries the held caution of someone who spent too long standing in the wrong room.
Chalk still clings to her fingertips and sleeve after lake water and canteen water alike; on the south road she rides rigid and careful, carrying Stratholme in her head before she ever sees it.

voiceMarkers.detail: No spoken dialogue on page in chapter 1; her silence and repeated off-page claim define her more than direct speech.
Sharp, impatient, and exact; she opens with the warning instead of apology, slips naturally into dense mage vocabulary, then bristles when Sylvanas cuts her back to plainer language.
Once Sylvanas proves she can follow the real argument, Jaina stops translating downward and speaks in her full register — longer, cleaner, and more precise.
Chapter 3 gives her the tone of a field scientist under duress: quiet, unsparing, and adamantly unwilling to call the loudest magical signal the true answer.
Her sarcasm with Sylvanas survives the horror intact, but now it comes after agreement on facts instead of in place of it.
Chapter 4 gives her a sharper bedside command voice: brisk questions, cutting corrections, and practical instructions delivered fast enough to outrun panic.

With Sylvanas she is still all bite and precision, but the rhythm has changed — she states what she can earn, absorbs Sylvanas's half of the answer immediately, and snaps back with sarcasm only after the shared picture lands.

Her Chapter 5 voice is fast, exact, and no longer trimmed down for Sylvanas. She speaks into the page and the air between them as if mutual comprehension is now the default, then corrects or accepts correction without wasting a syllable on pride.

Her Chapter 6 voice runs on sharpened clarity under humiliation: analytical when she describes the plague's design, cuttingly sarcastic when Arthas reduces weeks of evidence to 'one argument,' and stripped back to hard practical resolve once she turns from persuasion to proof.
Her Chapter 7 voice is brisk, exact, and unsentimental even under fatigue: the useful answer first, the mechanism in clean layers, and argument framed as how to keep both proof and momentum alive.
Her Chapter 8 voice stays exact under street pressure, now built for live movement as much as analysis: roofline counts, angle calls, and short interrogatives that turn fear into structure.

On the sending stone with Antonidas, she strips herself to the essential report — the name, the confidence level, the next direction — because the chapter has burned away any patience for explanation that cannot land.
Her chapter 9 voice goes flatter and more certain around ritual mechanics and trap calls, then drier and almost bloodless at the split.
In combat she reduces magical understanding into stripped survival commands: wait, back, left choke, two steps only when I move.

Chapter 12 gives her a seminar-speed voice sharpened into moral argument: she completes Kel'Thuzad's chains before he does, turns mechanism into consent and personhood, and keeps saying the clearest thing even when clarity wounds her more than uncertainty would.

Her most revealing line in the sanctum is not technical but diagnostic of herself: 'Understanding is not surrender ... but it is enough to wound.' She can now describe intellectual danger as precisely as magical danger.
Her spoken lines in chapter 13 are stripped to the road-useful core. She gives mechanism before explanation, answers in clean practical clauses, and leaves the sanctum truth unsaid.

Chapter 14 splits Jaina's voice between theorem-clean briefing and intimate omission: she can explain the plague in crisp operational clauses, answer Sylvanas's hard questions directly, and then reduce Antonidas to one devastating line — “He wasn't mad.”

beliefs.belief: Whatever she was doing at the ward-stones mattered enough that stopping would cost her the pattern.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as her insistence; the chapter does not yet prove whether she is right.

beliefs.belief: Something dangerous is moving north through the leylines and the stones can feel it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed and expanded in chapter 2: the danger is necromantic, accelerating, and no longer confined to the leyline lattice.

beliefs.belief: The contamination is deliberate death magic using the land's network itself and has already moved into soil, water, and living flesh.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by her readings and by the infected deer and wolves on the road.

beliefs.belief: The loudest magical reading is not automatically the source; false certainty would do as much damage as ignorance.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 3 when she refuses to call the granary the answer simply because it reads strongest.

beliefs.belief: Usable truth now has to be built from magic, spoor, and witness together.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 3; she explicitly supports Sylvanas's refusal to send warning on guesswork.

beliefs.belief: The plague enters living bodies through ingestion, with tainted grain and bread acting as the practical carrier inside ordinary routine.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 4.

beliefs.belief: The magical answer is only usable when it is laid beside logistics, diet, and the human pattern Sylvanas can read faster than she can.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 4.

beliefs.belief: The investigation only stays ahead of the plague if magical diagnosis revises the route as fast as new records and contamination depth demand.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 5 when Jaina repeatedly changes the chase order and the results keep sharpening the trail.

beliefs.belief: Sylvanas can follow the full argument, challenge the weak spot in it, and improve the map rather than merely obey its conclusions.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 5 through their shared map work and Sylvanas's correction of the feeder-road tally.

beliefs.belief: The plague was built by an intelligent magical mind using repetition, routine, and the body's own memory as part of the design.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by chapter 6's pattern lock; Jaina can now describe the work as layered necromantic engineering even if she still lacks the final architect's name.

beliefs.belief: Being right is useless if the listener values visible war over invisible catastrophe; proof may have to speak through the city itself instead of through authority.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by Arthas's refusal and by Jaina's final decision to make Andorhal the next proof target.

beliefs.belief: The city-scale answer only becomes usable when magical proof and field action move together; residue alone or capture alone leaves too much of the machine intact.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 7 when she and Sylvanas fold the residue read and the operative map into one same-night plan.

beliefs.belief: One live operative and one more clean layer of understanding might let her get ahead of the plague instead of chasing it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Disproved inside chapter 8: the witness dies mid-sentence and the route map shows the grain already beyond Andorhal.

beliefs.belief: The body is part of the plague's lock, and partial binding-sequence knowledge is still strategically useful even without the final animation trigger.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 9.

beliefs.belief: If she can move fast enough and read clearly enough, she can carry the Barov lead alone by replacing missing fieldcraft with rank, theory, and nerve.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Challenged in chapter 10 rather than fully broken. She gets farther in, but the chapter proves competence is not the same thing as safe solo judgment.

beliefs.belief: Necromancy is not an alien magical language but a continuous extension of the same arcane logic Dalaran taught her.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 by Kel'Thuzad's completed explanation and by Jaina's own ability to follow it all the way through.

beliefs.belief: Dalaran banned necromancy on ethical and civic grounds, not because the underlying theory failed.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12. Kel'Thuzad's argument is horrifying precisely because the theorem still works.

beliefs.belief: Her analytical mind is not its own moral guardrail; without outside friction, it can follow beautiful reasoning into atrocity before judgment catches up.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 when she sees the elegance in Kel'Thuzad's continuity model for one intolerable heartbeat and cannot unknow that she saw it.

beliefs.belief: The answer is only useful now if she can cut it down to what the road, the city, and Sylvanas can act on immediately.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by the way she briefs only the mechanism and the Stratholme implications once they are moving again.

beliefs.belief: Partial truth can keep the mission moving when the whole truth would break the formation at the wrong moment.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active but costly in chapter 14; the omission works tactically and further injures trust.

beliefs.belief: A narrow strike on feed points may blunt the next dose even if it cannot cure bodies already conditioned by the plague.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Tentative but supported by Jaina's chapter-14 synthesis of granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards as intervention points.

formerBeliefs.belief: If she can explain the contamination clearly enough, any sensible authority should act.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Millhaven forces her to admit that diagnosis alone is not enough; proof has to survive contact with field evidence and command consequences.

formerBeliefs.belief: The real answer will present itself first as a magical mechanism, with human routine and supply only supporting the analysis.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: The village only yields once Jaina lets bread, price, diet, and delivery history sit at the center of the method instead of at its edge.

formerBeliefs.belief: If she can lay the case out clearly enough to the right authority, rank and intelligence will force action.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Arthas hears the warning, understands the shape of the claim, and still chooses the visible war first. Clarity by itself does nothing.

formerBeliefs.belief: If the read is decisive enough, holding still in a hot site a little longer is a price the team can probably afford.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: The farmhouse ambush wounds Sylvanas and Lyriel dies at the compound; Jaina's old question of how long becomes operational cost rather than abstract pacing.

formerBeliefs.belief: If she can move fast enough and read clearly enough, she can carry the Barov lead alone by replacing missing fieldcraft with rank, theory, and nerve.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Chapter 12 proves solo access can win the theorem and still fail the deeper test. Jaina gets the answer, but only after discovering that absent human friction let her mind follow the logic farther than safety or judgment should have allowed.

goalsHistory.active: Explain the warning well enough to be heard before detention or politics buries it.
goalsHistory.underlying: Stop the northbound magical contamination she believes is already reaching Quel'Thalas.

goalsHistory.active: Get into Millhaven and identify how deep the contamination has taken hold before more time is lost.
goalsHistory.underlying: Prove the scope of the plague mechanism fast enough that action can still outrun it.

goalsHistory.active: Keep riding south with Sylvanas's reduced party and keep reading the wrongness before the next village falls all the way.
goalsHistory.underlying: Turn the Millhaven proof into a broader map of spread without pretending she has solved the vector yet.

goalsHistory.active: Ride southwest toward Andorhal with the grain records, sack marks, and confirmed ingestion pattern before more villages tip past saving.
goalsHistory.underlying: Get ahead of the distribution network now that she finally knows how the plague enters living bodies, even though she still does not know who built the system.

goalsHistory.active: Ride the south feeder road and read fresh grain teams before the network can clean farther ahead.
goalsHistory.underlying: Reach the Andorhal pinch-point quickly enough that the grain web yields structure instead of only aftermath.

goalsHistory.active: Enter Andorhal quietly and fast, learn the city's shape, and gather proof before the grain web can hide behind ordinary commerce.
goalsHistory.underlying: Force the plague to become undeniable somewhere evidence, traffic, and contamination all meet, since official sanction has failed.

goalsHistory.active: Support the same-night capture while preserving a readable residue trail in the repack rooms.
goalsHistory.underlying: Turn Andorhal from proof target into breach point before the district can scrub the treatment floor or close ranks around the cell.

goalsHistory.active: Turn Kel'Thuzad's name and the Andorhal proof into a moving Eastweald field campaign before more shipments disappear into ordinary bread.
goalsHistory.underlying: Trade perfect certainty for tempo, acting on knowledge that is incomplete but finally sufficient.

goalsHistory.active: Take the Barov route alone with the estate notes, route scraps, and incomplete ritual data from the farmhouse.
goalsHistory.underlying: Use academic cover and magical expertise to open a door the others cannot before the paper walls close completely.

goalsHistory.active: Leave the sanctum with the complete mechanism, the real scale of the threat, and enough composure to survive Sylvanas's arrival without lying to herself about what just happened in the room.
goalsHistory.underlying: Stop the plague with the full answer while refusing Kel'Thuzad's attempt to turn understanding into kinship.

goalsHistory.active: Ride south, keep the mechanism coherent enough to brief on the move, and get the road-relevant truth to Stratholme before time runs out.
goalsHistory.underlying: Carry the full sanctum knowledge without making the partnership break wider by trying to explain the room too early.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Reach Stratholme fast and turn the mechanism into action at granaries, bakehouses, and feed points before the city takes the next dose.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Keep the mission moving without forcing the sanctum room into the open before the road can bear it.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Exhausted, furious at not being heard, and still visibly fixed on the confiscated notes.
Shaken, physically wrung out, and still furious at the leash of custody, but newly forced into respect for Sylvanas's field judgment and the scale of what is coming.
Pale, spent, and still bad-tempered, but steadier in purpose now that Millhaven has turned suspicion into proof.
Exhausted, angry, and deeply frustrated by how little time the answer buys, but steadied by finally having a real mechanism and a road to follow.
Spent but sharpened: furious at the hidden handwork in the overseer's death, grimly energized by the clearer pattern, and disciplined enough to keep working after anger stops being useful.

Furious, humiliated, and physically hurting, but newly corrected: chapter 6 leaves her bitter about Arthas and steadier about what comes next, because she finally accepts that being right buys nothing without proof another power cannot ignore.

Tired, cold, and sharpened rather than humiliated: she has a clean mechanism answer, a city that validates weeks of warning, and a plan that depends on her work without requiring her to plead for its value.
Shocked, colder, and finally harder about time: Kel'Thuzad and Antonidas make the threat intimate, but the real correction is that clarity alone cannot save what has already moved.
Guilty, colder, and ruthlessly practical; Sylvanas's wound and Lyriel's death make every future analysis a timed risk rather than a pure search for truth.

Intellectually scorched, morally shaken, and held together by discipline rather than certainty. Relief at recognizing Sylvanas's approach arrives tangled with dread because whoever enters next will see aftermath without context.

Rigid, ashamed, and still disciplined enough to function. Relief at being out of Scholomance travels beside the knowledge that Sylvanas saw exactly the wrong pieces and had every reason to believe them.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Ashamed, exhausted, and tightly functional; the shame is still there, but chapter 14 steadies her once she can offer a road answer instead of a confession she cannot survive yet.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Crossed the ward line alone and kept working at the stones instead of fleeing when challenged.
Argued to continue the analysis even after detention rather than shifting into courtly self-protection.
Admitted outright that neither Antonidas nor the Kirin Tor sent her and that she crossed into Quel'Thalas on her own judgment.
Accepted armed escort and custody terms because proving the contamination mattered more than pride or freedom of movement.
After the wildlife attack, chose to give Sylvanas the full, unsoftened diagnosis and keep moving toward Millhaven rather than retreat into argument or self-justification.
Refused to call the granary the source just because it was the loudest reading, choosing honesty over the comfort of an answer.
Stayed in the village read despite visible magical backlash, then helped interpret the north road once Mira's testimony pointed that way.
Backed Sylvanas's demand for proof after the warning was sent, explicitly acknowledging the ranger had been right not to act on guesswork alone.
Forced her way into the sickroom assessment immediately and worked from living bodies instead of treating the village as already lost.
Built slowing wards for the salvageable cases even after realizing she had no cure, choosing triage over despair.
Compared symptom severity against diet and household routine until she could prove bread and grain were the carrier.
Moved on the granary with Sylvanas as soon as the two halves of the answer aligned, then chose pursuit toward Andorhal over staying to manage a village she could only partially help.
Redirected the initial paperwork chase away from the obvious road when repeated ingestion and older stock made a different route more revealing.
Abandoned bad assumptions quickly, sending the party back south the moment the newer evidence made the west fork secondary.
Investigated the stripped depot as a magical crime scene, identifying hidden necromantic residue in the overseer's staged death instead of accepting it as natural collapse.
Held the Barov clue at the level of paperwork and reach, refusing to inflate logistics evidence into proven noble guilt.
Kept the older grain seller in memory as a recurring anomaly without letting the callback derail the immediate road choice.
Presented the plague to Arthas as a deliberate grain-borne war and explicitly asked him to stop teams, seize records, and warn magistrates before Andorhal locked beyond saving.
Read Arthas's refusal as both personal wound and institutional failure, then chose not to spend another attempt on persuading him.
Accepted Sylvanas's harder reading of the room and redirected the quartet toward Andorhal as the proof target that would have to speak for itself.
Pushed through worsening magical pain on the city approach instead of stopping short, choosing arrival over recovery once Andorhal's pressure became undeniable.
Takes Corin and the mage-side access into the residue room and repack floor instead of insisting the whole team stay in one lane for safety.
Refuses the false choice between preserving proof and taking a prisoner, insisting on capture first with enough pre-marking to catch later tampering.
Suppresses the operative's collar ward at the instant of capture, keeping the city from hearing the grab too early.
Shifts from plague diagnosis to live pursuit-reading the moment the operative dies, feeding Sylvanas movement calls instead of trying to salvage a lost witness.
Uses Corin and Havel's fragments plus her own schooling to identify Kel'Thuzad rather than waiting for a cleaner confession that will never come.
Calls Antonidas immediately once the name lands, forcing the Kirin Tor thread to hear the truth before private shock can become delay.
Leaves Corin and Havel in Andorhal with split records and false trails while she commits the traveling unit toward Eastweald.
Stops the team at the farmhouse and commits to a deep read because the signature is recent enough to expose missing ritual architecture.
Disarms the threshold alarm line and stays in the work until the ambush physically tears her back out of it.
Carries away the useful half of the answer instead of pretending partial knowledge is failure.
Bandages Sylvanas twice under field pressure and then calls the halt that would have saved Lyriel if it had landed one heartbeat earlier.
Peels the trap layers open during retreat and accepts the solo Barov assignment once the split becomes unavoidable.
Chooses open Kirin Tor authority at the Barov gate instead of improvising stealth she does not actually possess.
Accepts Jandice's curated route because the strongest evidence is clearly ahead of the polite surface and she cannot afford to lose the lead to caution.
Reads the Scholomance research log despite recognizing Kel'Thuzad's intellectual lineage in it, choosing intelligence value over recoil.
Uses Jandice's pride as a lever, meeting her as a peer instead of only as a suspect, to win access to the lower levels.
Misleads Antonidas by promising safe extraction while deciding to go deeper the moment the line goes dead.
Completes Kel'Thuzad's plague logic on the board instead of refusing the reasoning, because getting the full mechanism matters more to her than the comfort of staying ignorant.
Forces the conversation away from elegant theory and onto consent, personhood, and civic violation the moment the mechanism becomes undeniable.
Does not lie to herself after the continuity model lands. She names the wound honestly instead of pretending comprehension would equal contamination or surrender.
Recognizes Sylvanas's approach through the ward cascades before the name is spoken and immediately understands that rescue will arrive with no context for what the room has done to her.
Turns Kel'Thuzad's room geometry into containment, sealing exits and leaving the northeast lane open for Sylvanas to use.
Chooses not to contest Sylvanas's misread inside the sanctum or on the island, giving only the mechanism they need to carry south.
Begins briefing the road version of the plague answer at once once Cyndia reports Hearthglen and Arthas's movement.
Briefs Sylvanas on the larger chain above the plague — the Lich King in Northrend and the Burning Legion above that — while keeping the sanctum's inner damage to herself.
Admits that Jandice curated her route and that Sylvanas saw workrooms and stock she herself never did, but still refuses the deeper confession about the room below.
Refuses Antonidas's recall and keeps riding south on purpose.
Turns the completed mechanism into a narrow field plan built around granaries, bakehouses, and main holding yards rather than pretending she already has a city-wide cure.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Field arcane analysis under pressure; laid her own spellwork over the ward runes to study the anomaly.
Endurance and obsessive focus strong enough to keep working past exhaustion, cold injury, and capture.
Frost-oriented magic use is implied by the damage on her knuckles.
Reads contamination through ward dampening, open leylines, soil, water, and dead flesh, distinguishing between the lattice carrying the damage and the land itself absorbing it.
Casts fast under fear: drops a low, wide frost sheet beneath charging wolves instead of wasting power on a slower direct strike.
Adapts her explanations to the listener when she stops assuming incomprehension, turning complex magical theory into actionable field guidance.
Separates saturation from source, distinguishing what is deepest from what is merely loudest in a contaminated environment.
Reads disturbed matter as magical evidence — older churn settling, fresher breaks still active — giving Sylvanas a second tracking language to compare against spoor.
Handles frightened survivors with gentler triage than Sylvanas, offering water, identity, and steadiness without taking command away from the ranger.
Maps contamination in living tissue quickly enough to identify a gut-first progression and separate heavier from lighter exposure by symptom pattern.
Uses stabilization wards as delay rather than cure, buying hours for patients who are not yet fully taken.
Reads a crafted necromantic concentrate as skilled applied work built to survive road, storehouse, oven, and repeated ingestion.
Uses contamination depth, delivery age, and dependence patterns to reprioritize which routes matter most in real time.
Detects deliberate necromantic residue hidden inside an apparently ordinary death, proving the enemy can bury magical signs beneath staged normalcy.
Works erased evidence back into legibility by pairing crystal light with physical scoring and route shorthand on scraped tally boards.
Absorbs correction without vanity and folds it straight back into the working map, making collaboration faster instead of more brittle.

Synthesizes road traffic, grain logistics, necromantic theory, and bodily symptoms into a coherent war-scale model of the plague without needing the final missing step to describe the threat accurately.
Can argue a complex magical case inside hostile command space without losing precision, even when personal history and rank pressure are working against her.
Feels proximity to the active grain hub somatically enough that Andorhal's corruption registers in her body before the city fully comes into view.
Isolates the hub-packaging mechanism from residue, dust, boards, and flare records, proving the grain is treated after milling during repack rather than spoiled earlier in the chain.
Works out of Sylvanas’s sightline and still returns with an answer precise enough to drive immediate field action.
Can smother a hostile ward-collar in motion, cutting off the cheap alarm trigger before it can ring the lane.
Reads fear directionally during interrogation, distinguishing terror of pain from terror aimed upward at a higher command.
Detects a remote necromantic silencing strike as a distinct, needle-fine intrusion rather than ambient plague residue.
Translates magical pressure into live escape guidance, tracking rooflines, street teams, and hostile casters quickly enough for Sylvanas to move on instinct.
Turns route sheets, district manifests, and magical residue into a regional deployment map, proving Andorhal is the throat of distribution rather than the final destination.
Reads layered binding work, alarm lines, and mass-animation architecture from a hot site fast enough to salvage actionable data under fire.
Transforms arcane insight into live tactical guidance, identifying anti-scout trap layers quickly enough to get the survivors out.
Performs practiced field bandaging under combat stress without losing analytical clarity.
Reads layered ward architecture across estate, lake, archive, and hidden stair, distinguishing presentation space from the deeper production core it is built over.
Recognizes Kirin Tor notation lineage instantly enough to identify Kel'Thuzad's methodological fingerprint before he appears in person.
Uses scholarly conversation itself as infiltration technique, baiting disclosure through rigor, curiosity, and peer-speed comprehension.
Can complete and stress-test plague theory at seminar speed, taking Kel'Thuzad's resonance model from partial field answer to full continuity architecture in live dialogue.

Can switch from theorem language to ethical language without losing precision, reframing the debate around consent, selfhood, and civic trust when the mathematics no longer help her resist the conclusion.
Reads layered ward disturbances well enough to identify a specific tactical approach through Scholomance before the intruder is visible.
Can convert existing ward lines and frost geometry into fast containment rather than raw force, shaping a room for Sylvanas's arrow instead of trying to overpower Kel'Thuzad directly.
Can compress catastrophic magical knowledge into immediate field-brief language while exhausted and under relational strain.
Compresses catastrophic magical knowledge into a moving field brief that a commander can act on immediately.
Maps a city-scale food network into actionable choke points under extreme time pressure.
Keeps analytical precision while carrying active shame, omitted truth, and the pressure of two refusing institutions at once.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Cyndia
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Functional labels until the papers confirm her identity.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Sylvanas
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Treats her as the prisoner and the mage; identity intensifies the threat rather than softening the address.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Sylvanas
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'mage' as both identifier and pressure point, with one pointed jab as 'the great magical authority of Dalaran.'

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Cyndia
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Shifts into escort-professional shorthand: 'Mage' when issuing quick riding or survival warnings.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Mira
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Learns her as 'Jaina' during the rescue, after most of the chapter has kept her as only 'the mage.'

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Dane Hall and the village medic
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Start by reading her as the visiting mage who can prove what the soldiers cannot, then fall into direct practical address once her authority is earned through action.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Arthas Menethil
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Opens with the polished public 'Lady Proudmoore,' shifts to the softer and more dangerous 'Jaina' when trying to make refusal sound reasonable.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Corin
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Starts in panicked deference with "lady" and a near-slip into "Archmage," which shows both Jaina’s stature and Corin’s relief at finally reaching someone who can read the same danger.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Antonidas
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Drops everything but her name on the stone; once Kel'Thuzad is spoken, the mentor cadence strips down into field urgency.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Sylvanas Windrunner
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses "Jaina" openly in assignment and argument, proof that her name has become working reflex rather than marked exception.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Kel'Thuzad
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'Jaina' once the exchange becomes true peer-speed argument, dropping rank and ceremony in favor of scholarly intimacy he thinks she has earned.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Kel'Thuzad
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Still uses formal praise and then direct intellectual regard, making his respect feel honest enough to wound the room even after Sylvanas arrives.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Antonidas
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Opens as “Jaina,” slips once into “Child” when fear breaks through, then lands back on her name as mentor rather than ruler.

howSheAddressesOthers.pattern: Avoids the name in direct address and defaults to clipped second-person replies, sarcasm, or formal challenge; the tonal shift is not in warmth but in dropping the condescending simplification.
Chapter 3 keeps the direct-address avoidance largely intact; she speaks in bare second person, then lets agreement land as acknowledgment rather than deference.
Introduces herself by name at once, using softness and plainness she does not offer Sylvanas.
Still mostly uses clipped second person, but chapter 4 adds a meaningful exception: she says 'Sylvanas' when the ledger and sack marks matter more than preserving distance.
Uses 'Arthas' without title, letting old intimacy and present contempt sit in the same address; by the refusal scene the lack of rank is its own judgment.
Uses his name plainly and immediately, calming him by moving him from rank panic into practical work.
Reports to him in hard field-brief language rather than as a sheltered apprentice, offering the name, the evidence chain, and the next vector with no rhetorical padding.
Chapter 8 keeps the clipped second-person habit, but now she obeys movement orders instantly and trades dry certainty with Sylvanas as if shared forward motion is already settled.
Chapter 9 uses Sylvanas's name in direct urgent warning and in the final split, with no trace of old distancing formality.
Speaks to him in exhausted practical terms, treating him as another pressured operator rather than ceremonial Silvermoon authority.
Uses his name directly once the mechanism is on the table, addressing him as a fellow trained mind she is actively refusing rather than as a distant monster category.
Chapter 13 is marked less by naming than by omission. Jaina answers orders, gives mechanism, and withholds the room itself.
Opens with “Archmage” for the report, then says “Antonidas” only once the call has become personal instead of procedural.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Sits upright and rigid without leaning back, staring at her confiscated notes rather than seeking sympathy from the room.
Looks up sharply at the sound of her own name, the fury in her jaw surviving visible exhaustion.
Leans forward when someone finally asks the right question, then keeps having to bite down on sharper answers she wants to throw back.
Throws herself physically into the work — kneeling in the road with one hand in the dirt, crouching over carcasses, bracing on staff or saddle whenever the magical surge hits too hard.
Stops at thresholds when the wrongness hits hardest, then forces herself through it anyway; in the granary she goes to one knee with the staff to the floor and rises only by catching a post.
After the dispatch leaves, she rides with the staff laid across the saddle bow rather than upright — a small visual concession to how much the place has drained her.

Braces on the trestle when the bread pattern locks, grips doorframes and thresholds on the way into the granary, and rubs the heel of her hand across her brow whenever fatigue threatens to show too plainly.
Moves toward danger before anyone can make a safer suggestion — through the granary door, down over the spill, and back into the hall for one more round of wards before she leaves.

Chapter 5 makes her work habits almost tactile: bent over copied tallies until her shoulder brushes Sylvanas by accident, one hand braced on the table when the pattern sharpens, and immediate physical lean-in whenever a correction might save them time.

Chapter 6 keeps translating strain into motion: fingers to her brow while the pattern locks, route sheet crushed against the board under Arthas's hand, shaking hidden against her mare's neck, then a deliberate look to Sylvanas for the harder read of the room before she gives the order to ride on.
Chapter 7 makes her work physically legible: sleeves rolled, hands planted on the table when the room tightens, and a stillness before choosing that reads as control rather than hesitation.
Waits between storehouses with the ward-thread stretched across metal and hinges, using stillness as part of the trap rather than as a pause before action.
Goes utterly still when the route map proves the grain has already moved beyond the city, the body freezing before the thought finishes.
After the stone call she straightens the route sheets once because her hands need work, then bends back over the map with a more ruthless practical focus.
Goes very still when the magical signature catches her, then shifts instantly into clipped kinetic precision once the fight starts.
Touches Sylvanas's sleeve to physically stop bad movement, then reties the bandage tighter at the split because care now lives inside argument, not apart from it.
Chapter 12 makes her scholar habits physically dangerous: she rises to the board, picks up the chalk without permission from herself, and leans into the proof before she remembers the room she is in.
When the full recurrence model lands, she jerks back hard enough to strike the chair into stone, then forces herself to sit again so Kel'Thuzad will not mistake recoil for collapse.
The doorway read lands through stillness: hand moving a fraction toward the staff and stopping, then later accepting the half-step escort without protest.
On the road south she speaks into hoofbeats instead of turning in the saddle, treating information as the only bridge she can risk laying down yet.
Keeps her eyes on ruts, ditch water, and the south road while briefing, as if looking at Sylvanas directly would make the omission break open.
Works the sending stone with her thumb pressed hard to the engraved edge, then fixes on the southern smoke as soon as the light dies.

knowledge.detail: Knows something is wrong in the leylines and believes the Thalassian ward-stones are already registering it.
Understands enough of the ward system to work diagnostic magic directly on the markers.
Knows the contamination behaves necromantically: it folds inward, clings instead of dissipating, and can ride the lattice between ward-stones without the stones themselves being corrupted.
Learns the spread is in ground water, root, and living tissue, not just in the ley lines, and that Millhaven is the first major knot where local currents pinch together.
Realizes Sylvanas read the confiscated map closely enough to understand the route and does not need the theory softened for her.
Confirms the whole village and north road are saturated, with the granary only the loudest point rather than a proven origin.
Learns Mira's testimony and the road read align: the dead rose after death and moved north in significant numbers.
Still does not know the original vector, though the newer grain seller enters her knowledge as a plausible weak clue rather than an answer.
Confirms that tainted grain and bread are the practical ingestion vector, with repeated meals worsening the body's load.
Learns the plague can be concentrated in liquid form before mixing and that the applied design sits beyond the approved necromantic education she received in Dalaran.
Finds recent sack marks and ledger notes that tie the active route southwest through Andorhal, making it the next visible node in the network.
Confirms that village-level operatives are disposable and disciplined enough to carry concealed poison for capture.
Knows the grain network is large enough that Andorhal reads as a knot in a regional web rather than a single suspect destination.
Learns the enemy is stripping depots ahead of them and can use hidden, precise necromantic handwork to remove a witness cleanly.
Understands the Barov seal as a logistics clue that may indicate ownership, lease, transfer hands, or infiltration, but not yet guilt.
Confirms the south feeder road is still carrying weight while the east line has gone quiet, making the route choice evidentiary rather than intuitive.
Adds one more remembered detail to the old grain-seller anomaly: the farewell "May the stars keep your road."
Understands the plague pattern now as deliberate war-scale design: grain as delivery shell, repeated ingestion as conditioning, and the body itself as part of the necromantic mechanism.
Learns that Arthas can hear a correct warning, partially credit it, and still subordinate it to the visible military emergency in front of him.
Confirms Andorhal is still outwardly alive and efficient — moving grain, smoke, carts, and gate traffic — while the same corrupt pressure battering her body hangs over it.
Learns that Andorhal’s grain is treated during hub repack with a preserving necromantic shell designed to survive handling and baking, while the host body still finishes the deeper work later.
Sees that the magical treatment and the human cell are one machine: the same city that hides the residue also hides a disciplined operative structure in the yards.
Learns that the city cells answer upward to one human architect and that the lower field necromancers are command-signal acolytes, not full designers of the plague.
Confirms the network can silence compromised operatives remotely with surgical precision inside the city.
Learns that Andorhal is only the throat of the deployment system, with treated grain already moving into bakeries, settlements, and garrison bread across northern Lordaeron.
Identifies Kel'Thuzad as the architect by stitching together the dead operative's broken clue, Corin's rumor, Havel's field fragments, and her own Kirin Tor memory.
Hears Antonidas confirm the name by silence and stripped urgency, which tells her the Kirin Tor thread is now running behind field time rather than ahead of it.
Confirms the recent ritual work preserves plague efficacy through storage and baking while leaving the final animation trigger missing.
Finds an estate-road mark pointing toward private stores, limited cycling hands, and Barov-country infrastructure rather than random rural ruin.
Learns the compound perimeter carries anti-intrusion wards calibrated to disciplined scout spacing.
Understands the latest Kirin Tor response has retreated into methodology review language even after Kel'Thuzad's name entered the field.
Confirms the Barov estate and Scholomance are live plague-support infrastructure, not dead rumor or abandoned archive.
Learns Jandice Barov is a genuine intellectual peer with Kirin Tor-adjacent training, laboratory scarring, and a need to have the work recognized.
Recognizes Antonidas's seminar notation carried forward in Kel'Thuzad's logs, making Scholomance feel like anti-Dalaran built from the same schooling rather than an alien system.
Adds resonance as the key concept organizing the missing animation trigger, while still lacking the full waking model.
Confirms a hidden lower research core exists beneath Jandice's authority and that Kel'Thuzad knows she is in the building.
By external Farstrider relay, Jaina entered the Barov estate, crossed to Caer Darrow before dusk, and did not return by nightfall.
Chapter 11 proves her absence has become an operational wound on Sylvanas’s road: the missing magical read is felt at every choke point, camp assessment, and route decision.

Now knows the complete resonance model and animation trigger: the grain is only the carrier shell, the body becomes the answering chamber, and low-fidelity village undead differ from deliberate preserved selfhood by degree and additional structure rather than by separate magical category.
Understands that Dalaran's necromancy ban was ethical and civic rather than mathematical; the theory still works, which is exactly why the line had to be moral.
Learns from Kel'Thuzad that the plague serves the Lich King in Northrend and that the Burning Legion sits above that chain as the larger invasion frame.
Recognizes that Sylvanas's absent perspective has been epistemic friction as much as physical protection; without that friction, her mind followed Kel'Thuzad's structure farther than she can forgive.
Now carries the complete practical statement of the mechanism in field language: grain carries the outer shell, the body learns the rest through exposure, and death lets it answer.
Also knows Sylvanas has filed the sanctum tableau as a real vulnerability and that any attempt to explain it immediately would sound like confirmation rather than repair.
Now knows Sylvanas came through hidden workrooms full of stock, breathing bodies, and separated parts that Jandice never showed her on the curated route.
Shares the full strategic chain with Sylvanas and Antonidas: the Lich King, the Burning Legion, the plague's body-learning mechanism, and Kel'Thuzad's expected lich return.
Learns from fresh relay traffic that Hearthglen nearly failed again after dark, Uther is still on the field, and Arthas is already racing Stratholme.
Identifies granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards as the best narrow intervention points left to them before Stratholme fully answers the grain.

arcHistory.currentPosition: She is still operating alone and paying the cost for it: physically exhausted, politically trapped, and dependent on whether Sylvanas chooses to hear her out.

The lie has cracked. She still hates the custody and still trusts her own mind first, but by the ride into Millhaven she is treating Sylvanas as necessary field intelligence rather than a blunt obstacle.
Chapter 3 turns her from sole diagnostician into one reader among several. She still supplies the magic, but she now openly treats Sylvanas's demand for proof as part of doing the job right.

Chapter 4 finishes the correction that chapter 3 began. Jaina still wants the clean mechanism, but she no longer pretends the human and logistical read is secondary to the magical one. The answer only locks when Sylvanas's half joins hers.

Chapter 5 completes Jaina's shift from solitary diagnostician to co-navigator. She is still the one reading contamination, but now she expects Sylvanas to keep pace with the full argument, accepts field correction instantly, and helps choose the road as part of a shared investigative method rather than as a trapped expert pleading to be believed.

Chapter 6 breaks the last clean version of Jaina's faith in explanation. She leaves Strahnbrad understanding that Sylvanas is not cynical but accurate, and that Andorhal's living surface may do more to prove the plague than any perfect briefing ever could.

Chapter 7 turns Jaina from corrected expert into autonomous half of the hunt. She no longer just accepts that Sylvanas can keep pace with the truth; now the chapter proves Jaina can work off-screen, return with the answer, and have that answer trusted at once.

Chapter 8 breaks Jaina's last clean fantasy that one more answer can become prevention. She still hunts knowledge, but now she prices it against time, loss, and movement instead of imagining clarity by itself will save the next thing in front of her.
Chapter 9 makes Jaina price knowledge against lives. She still seeks mechanism, but every deep read is now measured against blood loss, the dead ranger, and the fact that she must carry a lead alone.
Chapter 11 does not return to Jaina’s interior, but it hardens her narrative function as missing capability made urgent. Her silence, not her speech, drives Sylvanas’s final turn north.

Chapter 12 gives Jaina the full answer and makes that victory cost her the last safe illusion about intellect. She is still resisting Kel'Thuzad absolutely, but now she knows the theory works, knows her mind can see why it works, and knows the absent partner was part of what kept that recognition from happening sooner.

Chapter 12 finishes the mechanism hunt and opens a worse wound. Jaina now knows necromancy is continuous with her own education, knows Dalaran's line had to be ethical rather than theoretical, and no longer trusts intellect alone to keep her clean. Understanding survived. So did revulsion. That split is the chapter's scar.

Chapter 13 forces Jaina to carry truth and misread at once. She still has the answer, but now the cost of winning it lives not only in her own mind but in the damaged space it opens inside the partnership.

arc.lie: Being right should be enough to force people to listen, if only she can make the case clearly enough.
arc.truth: Knowledge only matters if it survives hierarchy, fear, and the cost in bodies required to win it.
arc.currentPosition: Chapter 14 makes the partial truth deliberate. Jaina chooses omission on purpose, then proves she is not only hiding — she is also converting the answer into the ugliest useful plan the road still allows. She now knows being right alone is the road toward Kel'Thuzad, not away from him.
